title: Median of two arrays
description: Find median of two sorted arrays using heaps

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize merged = [], i = 0, j = 0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2, append 1, i=1, merged=[1]. Step 3: i=1 (within nums1 length 2), j=0 (within nums2 length 1). Compare nums1[1]=3 and nums2[0]=2, append 2, j=1, merged=[1,2]. Step 4: j=1 (out of bounds for nums2), append remaining from nums1: nums1[1:]=[3], merged=[1,2,3]. Step 5: n=3 (odd), median = merged[3//2] = merged[1] = 2. Output: 2.0.

Step 1: Initialize merged = [], i = 0, j = 0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=3, append 1, i=1, merged=[1]. Step 3: i=1, j=0. Compare nums1[1]=2 and nums2[0]=3, append 2, i=2, merged=[1,2]. Step 4: i=2 (out of bounds for nums1), append remaining from nums2: nums2[0:]=[3,4], merged=[1,2,3,4]. Step 5: n=4 (even), median = (merged[4//2 - 1] + merged[4//2]) / 2 = (merged[1] + merged[2]) / 2 = (2 + 3) / 2 = 2.5. Output: 2.5.

approaches.name: Brute Force Merge O(m+n)
approaches.summary: Merge both sorted arrays into one combined sorted array, then find the median by accessing the middle element(s). This straightforward approach leverages the sorted property of input arrays but requires full merging.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Median of Two Sorted Arrays via Brute Force Merge
ladders.desc: Find the median of two sorted arrays by merging them into one sorted array and accessing the middle element(s).
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is straightforward: since both input arrays are already sorted, we can combine them into a single sorted array using a merge algorithm similar to merge sort. Once merged, finding the median becomes trivial—if the combined array has an odd length, the median is the middle element; if even, it's the average of the two middle elements. This works because merging preserves the sorted order, ensuring the median is correctly positioned. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list for the merged array and two pointers (i, j) at the start of each input array. 2) While both pointers are within bounds, compare elements at i and j, append the smaller to the merged list, and increment that pointer. 3) After one array is exhausted, append all remaining elements from the other array. 4) Compute the median: if the merged length is odd, return merged[n//2]; if even, return (merged[n//2 - 1] + merged[n//2]) / 2. Time complexity is O(m+n) due to traversing both arrays once, and space complexity is O(m+n) for storing the merged array. This feeds into the parent problem by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in median calculation (e.g., using integer division incorrectly) or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: def findMedianSortedArrays(nums1, nums2): merged = []; i = j = 0; while i < len(nums1) and j < len(nums2): if nums1[i] <= nums2[j]: merged.append(nums1[i]); i += 1 else: merged.append(nums2[j]); j += 1; merged.extend(nums1[i:]); merged.extend(nums2[j:]); n = len(merged); if n % 2 == 1: return merged[n//2]; else: return (merged[n//2 - 1] + merged[n//2]) / 2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize merged = [], i = 0, j = 0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2, append 1, i=1, merged=[1]. Step 3: i=1 (within nums1 length 2), j=0 (within nums2 length 1). Compare nums1[1]=3 and nums2[0]=2, append 2, j=1, merged=[1,2]. Step 4: j=1 (out of bounds for nums2), append remaining from nums1: nums1[1:]=[3], merged=[1,2,3]. Step 5: n=3 (odd), median = merged[3//2] = merged[1] = 2. Output: 2.0.

Step 1: Initialize merged = [], i = 0, j = 0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=3, append 1, i=1, merged=[1]. Step 3: i=1, j=0. Compare nums1[1]=2 and nums2[0]=3, append 2, i=2, merged=[1,2]. Step 4: i=2 (out of bounds for nums1), append remaining from nums2: nums2[0:]=[3,4], merged=[1,2,3,4]. Step 5: n=4 (even), median = (merged[4//2 - 1] + merged[4//2]) / 2 = (merged[1] + merged[2]) / 2 = (2 + 3) / 2 = 2.5. Output: 2.5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array using a two-pointer technique.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this sub-routine is to leverage the sorted nature of both arrays: by comparing the smallest unprocessed elements from each array, we can always pick the next smallest element for the merged array. This ensures the merged array remains sorted without needing to sort from scratch. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list for the merged array and two pointers (i, j) starting at 0 for each input array. 2) While both pointers are within their array bounds, compare the elements at i and j. 3) Append the smaller element to the merged list and increment that pointer. 4) Once one array is exhausted, append all remaining elements from the other array to the merged list. Time complexity is O(m+n) as each element is processed once, and space complexity is O(m+n) for the merged array. This feeds into L0 by providing the merged array needed for median calculation. Common mistakes include not handling cases where one array is empty or incorrectly updating pointers. Real code pattern: def mergeSortedArrays(nums1, nums2): merged = []; i = j = 0; while i < len(nums1) and j < len(nums2): if nums1[i] <= nums2[j]: merged.append(nums1[i]); i += 1 else: merged.append(nums2[j]); j += 1; merged.extend(nums1[i:]); merged.extend(nums2[j:]); return merged.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize merged = [], i = 0, j = 0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2, append 1, i=1, merged=[1]. Step 3: i=1, j=0. Compare nums1[1]=3 and nums2[0]=2, append 2, j=1, merged=[1,2]. Step 4: i=1, j=1. Compare nums1[1]=3 and nums2[1]=4, append 3, i=2, merged=[1,2,3]. Step 5: i=2 (out of bounds for nums1), append remaining from nums2: nums2[1:]=[4], merged=[1,2,3,4]. Output: [1, 2, 3, 4].

Step 1: Initialize merged = [], i = 0, j = 0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=5 and nums2[0]=1, append 1, j=1, merged=[1]. Step 3: i=0, j=1. Compare nums1[0]=5 and nums2[1]=2, append 2, j=2, merged=[1,2]. Step 4: j=2 (out of bounds for nums2), append remaining from nums1: nums1[0:]=[5,6], merged=[1,2,5,6]. Output: [1, 2, 5, 6].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pointer Comparison for Merging
ladders.desc: Use two pointers to traverse sorted arrays and compare elements to build a merged sorted array.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this core logic is that since both arrays are sorted, the smallest overall element must be at one of the two current pointer positions. By repeatedly comparing these elements, we can sequentially build the merged array in sorted order. This technique is efficient because it avoids redundant comparisons and processes each element exactly once. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Set pointers i and j to 0 for arrays A and B. 2) While i < len(A) and j < len(B), compare A[i] and B[j]. 3) If A[i] <= B[j], add A[i] to the result and increment i; otherwise, add B[j] and increment j. 4) This loop continues until one array is fully processed. Time complexity is O(min(m, n)) for the comparison loop, but overall O(m+n) when combined with appending remainders. Space complexity is O(1) extra space if not counting the output array. This feeds into L1 by forming the main loop of the merge subroutine. Common mistakes include using strict inequality (< instead of <=) which can cause instability or incorrect ordering when elements are equal. Real code pattern: while i < len(A) and j < len(B): if A[i] <= B[j]: result.append(A[i]); i += 1 else: result.append(B[j]); j += 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0. Compare A[0]=1 and B[0]=2, since 1 <= 2, append 1, i=1. Step 2: i=1, j=0. Compare A[1]=3 and B[0]=2, since 3 > 2, append 2, j=1. Loop ends as i=1 (< len(A)=2) and j=1 (< len(B)=2), but we stop trace here for demonstration. Output: Merged prefix: [1, 2] with i=1, j=1.

Step 1: i=0, j=0. Compare A[0]=5 and B[0]=1, since 5 > 1, append 1, j=1. Step 2: i=0, j=1. Compare A[0]=5 and B[1]=2, since 5 > 2, append 2, j=2. Loop ends as j=2 (>= len(B)=2). Output: Merged prefix: [1, 2] with i=0, j=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Appending Remainders After Merge Loop
ladders.desc: After the two-pointer comparison loop, append all remaining elements from the non-exhausted array to the merged list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this building block is that once one array is fully processed by the comparison loop, the other array's remaining elements are already sorted and all larger than the last appended element, so they can be added directly without further comparison. This ensures completeness of the merged array. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) After the while loop (which stops when i >= len(A) or j >= len(B)), check which array has unprocessed elements. 2) If i < len(A), append all elements from A[i:] to the result. 3) If j < len(B), append all elements from B[j:] to the result. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of remaining elements, contributing to overall O(m+n). Space complexity is O(1) extra if appending in-place. This feeds into L2 by handling the tail end of the merge process. Common mistakes include appending from the wrong index or forgetting to handle both cases. Real code pattern: if i < len(A): result.extend(A[i:]); if j < len(B): result.extend(B[j:]).

examples.trace: Step 1: After comparison loop, result=[1,2], i=1, j=1. Step 2: Check i=1 < len(A)=2, so append A[1:]=[3], result=[1,2,3]. Step 3: Check j=1 < len(B)=2, so append B[1:]=[4], result=[1,2,3,4]. Output: result=[1,2,3,4].

Step 1: After comparison loop, result=[1,2], i=0, j=2. Step 2: Check i=0 < len(A)=2, so append A[0:]=[5,6], result=[1,2,5,6]. Step 3: Check j=2 >= len(B)=2, so no append from B. Output: result=[1,2,5,6].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Append Elements
ladders.desc: Compare two elements from different arrays and append the smaller one to a result list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this basic operation is that in a merge process, we need to decide which element comes next in sorted order. By comparing two candidate elements, we can always pick the smaller (or equal) one to maintain ascending order. This operation is fundamental because it's repeated in the merge loop. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Given two elements a and b from arrays A and B at current indices. 2) If a <= b, append a to the result and increment the index for A. 3) Otherwise, append b and increment the index for B. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison, and space complexity is O(1) if appending to an existing list. This feeds into L3 by being the core action within the comparison loop. Common mistakes include using < instead of <=, which can lead to incorrect ordering when elements are equal, or not updating indices correctly. Real code pattern: if a <= b: result.append(a); i += 1 else: result.append(b); j += 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare a=1 and b=2. Since 1 <= 2, append 1 to result, so result=[1]. Increment i from 0 to 1. j remains 0. Output: result=[1], i=1, j=0.
Step 1: Compare a=5 and b=1. Since 5 > 1, append 1 to result, so result=[1]. Increment j from 0 to 1. i remains 0. Output: result=[1], i=0, j=1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its index position.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for this foundation concept is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element can be accessed directly using its index (position). This is crucial for the merge approach because we need to compare and append specific elements from the input arrays. In programming, indices typically start at 0, so the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Given an array arr and an index i. 2) Check that i is within the valid range: 0 <= i < len(arr). 3) Retrieve the element as arr[i]. Time complexity is O(1) for direct access, and space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L4 by enabling the comparison of elements a and b. Common mistakes include using indices out of bounds (e.g., negative or >= len(arr)), which can cause errors, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: if 0 <= i < len(arr): element = arr[i] else: handle error (e.g., return None or raise exception).

examples.trace: Step 1: Array arr has length 3, indices range from 0 to 2. Step 2: Check i=1 is within 0 <= 1 < 3, so valid. Step 3: Access arr[1], which is the second element (since indexing starts at 0). Output: 20.
Step 1: Array arr has length 1, indices range from 0 to 0. Step 2: Check i=0 is within 0 <= 0 < 1, so valid. Step 3: Access arr[0], which is the first element. Output: 5.

approaches.name: Two Heaps (Min-Max) O((m+n) log k)
approaches.summary: Use a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half of elements from both arrays, maintaining balance to efficiently find the median. This approach processes elements incrementally using heap operations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Median of Two Sorted Arrays Using Two Heaps
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find median of two sorted arrays using min-max heaps.
ladders.explanation: This approach uses two heaps to maintain a running median as we process elements from both sorted arrays. The intuition is to simulate merging the arrays while keeping track of the median without actually merging them fully. We maintain a max-heap for the lower half of elements and a min-heap for the upper half. At any point, the median can be computed from the tops of these heaps. The key insight is that by processing elements in sorted order (using the fact that both arrays are sorted), we can insert elements into the appropriate heap while maintaining balance between the heaps. This allows us to compute the median in O((m+n) log k) time where k is the number of elements processed so far. The algorithm works by using two pointers to traverse both arrays, always picking the smaller current element from either array. For each element, we add it to the appropriate heap based on its value relative to current heap tops, then rebalance the heaps to ensure the size difference is at most 1. After processing all elements, the median is computed based on whether total elements are even or odd. The space complexity is O(m+n) to store all elements in the heaps. Common mistakes include forgetting to rebalance after each insertion, incorrectly handling the case when one heap is empty, and not properly computing the median for even vs odd counts. This approach is particularly useful when you need to process streaming data or when you want to avoid the O(m+n) space of full merging.

examples.trace:
Initialize: max_heap = [], min_heap = [], i = 0, j = 0
Step 1: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2, pick 1
  Add to max_heap: max_heap = [1], min_heap = []
  Rebalance: sizes equal (1,0), no rebalance needed
Step 2: i=1, j=0, compare nums1[1]=3 and nums2[0]=2, pick 2
  Add to min_heap (since 2 > max_heap.top=1): min_heap = [2], max_heap = [1]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,1), balanced
Step 3: i=1, j=1, only nums1[1]=3 remains
  Add to min_heap (since 3 > max_heap.top=1): min_heap = [2,3], max_heap = [1]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,2), need to move from min to max: max_heap = [1,2], min_heap = [3]
All elements processed. Total count = 3 (odd)
Median = max_heap.top = 2.0

Initialize: max_heap = [], min_heap = [], i = 0, j = 0
Step 1: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=3, pick 1
  Add to max_heap: max_heap = [1], min_heap = []
  Rebalance: sizes (1,0), balanced
Step 2: i=1, j=0, compare nums1[1]=2 and nums2[0]=3, pick 2
  Add to max_heap (since 2 <= max_heap.top=1? No, 2 > 1): add to min_heap: min_heap = [2], max_heap = [1]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,1), balanced
Step 3: i=2, j=0, only nums2[0]=3 remains
  Add to min_heap (since 3 > max_heap.top=1): min_heap = [2,3], max_heap = [1]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,2), move from min to max: max_heap = [1,2], min_heap = [3]
Step 4: i=2, j=1, only nums2[1]=4 remains
  Add to min_heap (since 4 > max_heap.top=2): min_heap = [3,4], max_heap = [1,2]
  Rebalance: sizes (2,2), balanced
All elements processed. Total count = 4 (even)
Median = (max_heap.top + min_heap.top)/2 = (2 + 3)/2 = 2.5

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Balanced Heaps While Processing Sorted Elements
ladders.desc: Implement the core heap maintenance logic that processes elements from two sorted arrays.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the main loop of the algorithm where we process elements from both sorted arrays while maintaining two balanced heaps. The intuition is that since both arrays are sorted, we can use a two-pointer approach to always pick the next smallest element from either array. For each element, we need to insert it into the appropriate heap and then rebalance the heaps. The key insight is that we can determine which heap to insert into by comparing the element with the current tops of the heaps. If the element is less than or equal to the top of the max-heap (which contains the lower half), it belongs in the max-heap. Otherwise, it belongs in the min-heap. After insertion, we must rebalance to ensure that the size difference between the heaps is at most 1, with the max-heap potentially having one more element when total count is odd. This rebalancing is done by moving elements from the larger heap to the smaller one. The time complexity for processing n elements is O(n log n) due to heap operations. The space complexity is O(n) for storing elements in heaps. Common mistakes include not handling empty heaps when comparing with tops, incorrect rebalancing logic, and off-by-one errors in size comparisons. This sub-routine directly feeds into the full problem by providing the mechanism to process all elements while maintaining the data structure needed for median computation.

examples.trace:
Start: max_heap = [], min_heap = []
Process 1: max_heap empty, add to max_heap → max_heap = [1]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,0) → balanced
Process 2: compare with max_heap.top=1, 2 > 1 → add to min_heap → min_heap = [2]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,1) → balanced
Process 3: compare with max_heap.top=1, 3 > 1 → add to min_heap → min_heap = [2,3]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,2) → move min_heap.top=2 to max_heap
    max_heap = [2,1], min_heap = [3]
  Rebalance: sizes (2,1) → balanced
Final: max_heap = [2,1], min_heap = [3]

Start: max_heap = [], min_heap = []
Process 1: max_heap empty → add to max_heap → max_heap = [1]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,0) → balanced
Process 2: compare with max_heap.top=1, 2 > 1 → add to min_heap → min_heap = [2]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,1) → balanced
Process 3: compare with max_heap.top=1, 3 > 1 → add to min_heap → min_heap = [2,3]
  Rebalance: sizes (1,2) → move min_heap.top=2 to max_heap
    max_heap = [2,1], min_heap = [3]
  Rebalance: sizes (2,1) → balanced
Process 4: compare with max_heap.top=2, 4 > 2 → add to min_heap → min_heap = [3,4]
  Rebalance: sizes (2,2) → balanced
Process 5: compare with max_heap.top=2, 5 > 2 → add to min_heap → min_heap = [3,4,5]
  Rebalance: sizes (2,3) → move min_heap.top=3 to max_heap
    max_heap = [3,1,2], min_heap = [4,5]
  Rebalance: sizes (3,2) → balanced
Final: max_heap = [3,1,2], min_heap = [4,5]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Median Computation from Balanced Heaps
ladders.desc: Compute median from two heaps containing lower and upper halves of elements.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on how to compute the median once we have two balanced heaps containing all elements. The intuition is that the max-heap contains the lower half of elements (with the maximum at the top), and the min-heap contains the upper half (with the minimum at the top). When the total number of elements is odd, the median is simply the top of the max-heap (since we maintain it to have one more element when counts are odd). When the total is even, the median is the average of the tops of both heaps. The key insight is that by keeping the heaps balanced with size difference at most 1, we can always access the middle element(s) in O(1) time. The algorithm checks the sizes of both heaps: if they're equal, return (max_heap.top + min_heap.top)/2; otherwise, return max_heap.top. This works because we maintain the invariant that all elements in max_heap are <= all elements in min_heap, and the sizes are properly balanced. Time complexity is O(1) for this computation. Space complexity is O(1) additional space. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the case when one heap is empty (which only happens when total count is 1), incorrectly computing the average for even counts, and not converting to float when necessary. This logic is essential for the full problem as it provides the final answer after all elements are processed.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check sizes: max_heap size = 3, min_heap size = 2
Step 2: Since sizes are not equal (3 ≠ 2), total is odd
Step 3: For odd total, median = top of larger heap (max_heap)
Step 4: max_heap.top = 3 (after heap property, root is max)
Step 5: Return 3.0 as float

Step 1: Check sizes: max_heap size = 2, min_heap size = 2
Step 2: Since sizes are equal, total is even
Step 3: For even total, median = (max_heap.top + min_heap.top)/2
Step 4: max_heap.top = 2, min_heap.top = 3
Step 5: Compute (2 + 3)/2 = 2.5
Step 6: Return 2.5

ladders.title: Building Block — Rebalancing Two Heaps After Insertion
ladders.desc: Maintain balance between max-heap and min-heap after inserting a new element.
ladders.explanation: This building block handles the crucial rebalancing step that must occur after each insertion into the heaps. The intuition is that to efficiently compute medians, we need to maintain the invariant that the size difference between the max-heap (lower half) and min-heap (upper half) is at most 1. Additionally, we must maintain that all elements in the max-heap are less than or equal to all elements in the min-heap. After inserting an element into either heap, we check the sizes: if one heap has more than one extra element compared to the other, we move the root element from the larger heap to the smaller heap. This rebalancing ensures that the heaps always represent the lower and upper halves of the elements processed so far. The key insight is that moving the root maintains both the heap property and the ordering invariant between heaps. For example, if we move the max from the max-heap to the min-heap, it becomes the new minimum in the min-heap, preserving the ordering. The time complexity is O(log n) for the heap operations during rebalancing. Space complexity is O(1) additional space. Common mistakes include rebalancing even when sizes are already balanced, incorrect comparison for when to rebalance (should be size difference > 1), and not updating both heaps properly when moving elements. This building block is essential for the core logic as it maintains the data structure integrity needed for median computation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check sizes: max_heap size = 3, min_heap size = 1
Step 2: Size difference = |3-1| = 2 > 1, so need rebalancing
Step 3: max_heap is larger, so move its max to min_heap
Step 4: Extract max from max_heap: max = 5
  max_heap becomes [3,1] after extraction
Step 5: Insert 5 into min_heap: min_heap becomes [5,6]
Step 6: Check sizes again: max_heap size = 2, min_heap size = 2
Step 7: Size difference = 0 ≤ 1, so balanced

Step 1: Check sizes: max_heap size = 1, min_heap size = 3
Step 2: Size difference = |1-3| = 2 > 1, so need rebalancing
Step 3: min_heap is larger, so move its min to max_heap
Step 4: Extract min from min_heap: min = 2
  min_heap becomes [3,4] after extraction
Step 5: Insert 2 into max_heap: max_heap becomes [2,1]
Step 6: Check sizes again: max_heap size = 2, min_heap size = 2
Step 7: Size difference = 0 ≤ 1, so balanced

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Insert Element into Appropriate Heap Based on Current State
ladders.desc: Decide whether to insert a new element into max-heap or min-heap.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation determines which heap to insert a new element into based on the current state of the heaps. The intuition is that we want to maintain the invariant that all elements in the max-heap (lower half) are less than or equal to all elements in the min-heap (upper half). When we have a new element to insert, we compare it with the current tops of the heaps to decide where it belongs. If the max-heap is empty or the element is less than or equal to the top of the max-heap, it belongs in the max-heap (lower half). Otherwise, it belongs in the min-heap (upper half). The key insight is that this decision preserves the ordering between heaps. For example, if we insert an element into the max-heap that's greater than its current top, the heap property will rearrange it, but it will still be ≤ elements in the min-heap because we only insert into max-heap when the element is ≤ current max-heap top. The time complexity is O(1) for the comparison plus O(log n) for the heap insertion. Space complexity is O(1) additional space. Common mistakes include not handling empty heaps (need to check if heap is empty before accessing top), incorrect comparison logic, and forgetting that we compare with max-heap top only (not min-heap top). This operation is fundamental to the building block as it's the first step before rebalancing.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check if max_heap is empty? No
Step 2: Compare element 4 with max_heap.top = 3
Step 3: 4 > 3, so element does NOT belong in max_heap
Step 4: Therefore, insert into min_heap
Step 5: min_heap becomes [4,5] after insertion

Step 1: Check if max_heap is empty? No
Step 2: Compare element 2 with max_heap.top = 5
Step 3: 2 <= 5, so element belongs in max_heap
Step 4: Insert into max_heap
Step 5: max_heap becomes [5,3,2] after insertion

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Min-Heap and Max-Heap Properties
ladders.desc: Learn the basic properties and operations of min-heaps and max-heaps.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the fundamental data structures of min-heaps and max-heaps, which are complete binary trees that satisfy the heap property. In a min-heap, every parent node has a value less than or equal to its children, so the minimum element is at the root. In a max-heap, every parent node has a value greater than or equal to its children, so the maximum element is at the root. The intuition is that heaps allow efficient access to the minimum or maximum element in O(1) time, and efficient insertion and extraction in O(log n) time. Key operations include: insert(value) which adds a new element and restores heap property by bubbling up; extract_min() or extract_max() which removes the root and restores heap property by bubbling down; peek() which returns the root without removal. Heaps are typically implemented using arrays where for index i, left child is at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2, and parent at floor((i-1)/2). The time complexity for insertion and extraction is O(log n) where n is the number of elements, and O(1) for peek. Space complexity is O(n) to store all elements. Common mistakes include confusing min-heap with max-heap, incorrect index calculations in array implementation, and not maintaining the complete tree property. This foundation is essential for all higher levels as the two-heap approach relies entirely on these heap properties and operations to efficiently maintain and access median elements.

examples.trace:
Start: empty heap []
Insert 5: heap = [5]
Insert 3: add to end [5,3], bubble up: 3 < 5, swap → [3,5]
Insert 8: add to end [3,5,8], bubble up: 8 ≥ 3, no swap
Insert 1: add to end [3,5,8,1], bubble up: 1 < 5, swap → [3,1,8,5]; 1 < 3, swap → [1,3,8,5]
Final min-heap: [1,3,8,5] (root=1 is minimum)

Start: empty heap []
Insert 5: heap = [5]
Insert 3: add to end [5,3], bubble up: 3 ≤ 5, no swap
Insert 8: add to end [5,3,8], bubble up: 8 > 5, swap → [8,3,5]
Insert 1: add to end [8,3,5,1], bubble up: 1 ≤ 3, no swap
Final max-heap: [8,3,5,1] (root=8 is maximum)

approaches.name: Heap Merge for Kth Element O(k log m)
approaches.summary: Use a min-heap to merge arrays and extract the k-th smallest elements to find the median, stopping early once the median position is reached. This optimizes by not processing all elements if unnecessary.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Median of Two Sorted Arrays Using Heap Merge
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using min-heap to merge arrays and extract k-th smallest elements to find median.
ladders.explanation: This solution finds the median of two sorted arrays by leveraging a min-heap to efficiently merge them while only processing up to the median position. The intuition is that we don't need to fully merge both arrays—we only need to reach the middle element(s). For arrays of total length n, the median is at position k = n//2 (for odd n) or average of positions k-1 and k (for even n). We use a min-heap to always extract the next smallest element from both arrays. We maintain a heap of size at most 2 (one element from each array's current position) to track the next candidates. At each step, we pop the smallest element and increment its source array's pointer. We stop after extracting k elements for odd n, or k elements for even n (then need both k-1 and k). The algorithm: 1. Calculate total length n and median position k. 2. Initialize heap with first elements of both arrays (if non-empty). 3. Initialize pointers i=0, j=0 for arrays. 4. For count from 0 to k: a. Pop smallest from heap. b. If count == k-1 for even n, store as first median candidate. c. If count == k for odd n, return popped value as median. d. Push next element from the same array (if exists) to heap. 5. For even n, average last two popped values. Time: O(k log 2) = O(k) since heap size ≤ 2, but actually O(k log m) where m=2 in this case. Space: O(1) for heap plus pointers. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into complete solution. Common mistakes: Not handling empty arrays, incorrect median position calculation, forgetting to push next element after pop. Real pattern: Use heapq in Python with tuples (value, array_index, element_index).

examples.trace: Total length n=3, odd, k=1 (0-indexed). Heap init: [(1,0,0), (2,1,0)]. Count=0: pop (1,0,0), value=1, count≠k, push next from nums1: (3,0,1). Heap: [(2,1,0), (3,0,1)]. Count=1: pop (2,1,0), value=2, count=k, return 2.0.

Total n=4, even, k=2 (need positions 1 and 2, 0-indexed). Heap init: [(1,0,0), (3,1,0)]. Count=0: pop (1,0,0), value=1, store first=None (not k-1), push next from nums1: (2,0,1). Heap: [(2,0,1), (3,1,0)]. Count=1: pop (2,0,1), value=2, store first=2 (count=k-1), push next from nums1: none. Heap: [(3,1,0)]. Count=2: pop (3,1,0), value=3, count=k, median=(2+3)/2=2.5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Extract K-th Smallest Element Using Min-Heap Merge
ladders.desc: Helper function that merges two sorted arrays using a min-heap to find the k-th smallest element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the median solution—it finds the k-th smallest element (0-indexed) from two sorted arrays without fully merging them. The intuition: since arrays are sorted, we can use a min-heap to always track the next smallest element from both arrays. We only need to extract k+1 elements total (for k-th element). The algorithm: 1. Initialize a min-heap. 2. Push the first element of each array (if exists) as tuples (value, array_index, element_index). 3. Initialize pointers i=0, j=0 (but tracked via heap). 4. For count from 0 to k: a. Pop smallest element from heap. b. If count == k, return this value as k-th smallest. c. Otherwise, push the next element from the same array (increment pointer) to heap. 5. If heap empties before reaching k, handle error (shouldn't happen with valid k). Time: O(k log 2) = O(k) since heap has at most 2 elements. Space: O(1) for heap. Connection: This directly feeds into L0 by providing the k-th element needed for median. Common mistakes: Forgetting to push next element after pop, incorrect tuple ordering in heap, not handling when one array is exhausted. Real pattern: Use heapq.heappush/pop with (value, array_id, index).

examples.trace: Heap init: [(1,0,0), (2,1,0)]. Count=0: pop (1,0,0), value=1, count≠k, push next from nums1: (3,0,1). Heap: [(2,1,0), (3,0,1)]. Count=1: pop (2,1,0), value=2, count≠k, push next from nums2: (4,1,1). Heap: [(3,0,1), (4,1,1)]. Count=2: pop (3,0,1), value=3, count=k, return 3.
Heap init: [(1,0,0), (3,1,0)]. Count=0: pop (1,0,0), value=1, count≠k, push next from nums1: (2,0,1). Heap: [(2,0,1), (3,1,0)]. Count=1: pop (2,0,1), value=2, count=k, return 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Merging Two Sorted Arrays with a Min-Heap
ladders.desc: Technique to merge two sorted arrays by always picking the smallest next element using a heap.
ladders.explanation: This core logic demonstrates how to merge two sorted arrays efficiently using a min-heap. The intuition: instead of comparing front elements directly (which would be O(1) but requires manual tracking), we use a heap to automatically always give us the smallest available element. This generalizes to merging m arrays, but here m=2. The algorithm: 1. Create empty min-heap. 2. Push first elements of both arrays (if exist) as (value, array_index, element_index). 3. While heap not empty: a. Pop smallest element. b. Add to merged result. c. Push next element from same array (if exists). This yields fully merged sorted array. Time: O(n log 2) = O(n) for n total elements. Space: O(1) for heap. Connection: This is simplified version of L1—instead of stopping at k, we merge all elements. Common mistakes: Not using tuples to track array source, infinite loop if not pushing next elements correctly. Real pattern: heapq with while loop.

examples.trace: Heap init: [(1,0,0), (2,1,0)]. Step1: pop 1, result=[1], push next from nums1: (3,0,1). Heap: [(2,1,0), (3,0,1)]. Step2: pop 2, result=[1,2], push next from nums2: (4,1,1). Heap: [(3,0,1), (4,1,1)]. Step3: pop 3, result=[1,2,3], no next from nums1. Heap: [(4,1,1)]. Step4: pop 4, result=[1,2,3,4], done.

Heap init: [(5,0,0), (1,1,0)]. Step1: pop 1, result=[1], push next from nums2: (6,1,1). Heap: [(5,0,0), (6,1,1)]. Step2: pop 5, result=[1,5], push next from nums1: (10,0,1). Heap: [(6,1,1), (10,0,1)]. Step3: pop 6, result=[1,5,6], no next from nums2. Heap: [(10,0,1)]. Step4: pop 10, result=[1,5,6,10], done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Min-Heap Operations for Multiple Sequences
ladders.desc: Using a min-heap to manage the smallest elements from multiple sorted sequences.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept shows how a min-heap can track the smallest current elements from multiple sequences (here, two arrays). The intuition: a heap lets us efficiently always get the minimum among current candidates without scanning all sequences each time. We store tuples (value, sequence_id, index) so we know which sequence the value came from and can fetch the next element from that sequence. The algorithm: 1. Initialize empty min-heap. 2. For each sequence, if non-empty, push (first_element, sequence_id, 0). 3. While heap not empty: a. Pop smallest tuple. b. Process value (e.g., add to output). c. If sequence has next element, push (next_value, same_sequence_id, next_index). Time: O(n log m) for n total elements and m sequences. Space: O(m) for heap. Connection: This is the mechanism behind L2's merging. Common mistakes: Forgetting to include sequence_id in tuple, not handling empty sequences, incorrect heap ordering. Real pattern: heapq with tuple elements.

examples.trace: Heap init: [(1,0,0), (2,1,0)]. Step1: pop (1,0,0), value=1, push next from seq0: (4,0,1). Heap: [(2,1,0), (4,0,1)]. Step2: pop (2,1,0), value=2, push next from seq1: (3,1,1). Heap: [(3,1,1), (4,0,1)]. Step3: pop (3,1,1), value=3, no next from seq1. Heap: [(4,0,1)]. Step4: pop (4,0,1), value=4, done.

Heap init: [(5,0,0), (1,1,0)]. Step1: pop (1,1,0), value=1, push next from seq1: (6,1,1). Heap: [(5,0,0), (6,1,1)]. Step2: pop (5,0,0), value=5, no next from seq0. Heap: [(6,1,1)]. Step3: pop (6,1,1), value=6, done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Min-Heap Push and Pop with Tuples
ladders.desc: Fundamental heap operations using tuples to store additional metadata.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers how to use a min-heap (priority queue) with tuples, which is essential for tracking elements from different sources. The intuition: a heap in Python (using heapq) orders tuples lexicographically—first by first element, then second, etc. So we can store (value, source_id, index) to break ties and identify source. Key operations: heapq.heappush(heap, item) adds item maintaining heap order; heapq.heappop(heap) removes and returns smallest item. For tuples, smallest means smallest first element; if equal, compare second, etc. The algorithm: 1. Import heapq. 2. Initialize empty list as heap. 3. Push tuples: heapq.heappush(heap, (value, id, idx)). 4. Pop: val, id, idx = heapq.heappop(heap). Time: O(log n) per operation for heap size n. Space: O(n). Connection: This enables L3's heap management. Common mistakes: Pushing non-tuples, expecting max-heap (use negative values), not importing heapq. Real pattern: import heapq; heap=[]; heapq.heappush(heap, (5,0,0)); val,id,idx=heapq.heappop(heap).

examples.trace: Heap init: []. Step1: push (3,0,0), heap=[(3,0,0)]. Step2: push (1,1,0), heap reorders to [(1,1,0), (3,0,0)]. Step3: push (2,0,1), heap reorders to [(1,1,0), (3,0,0), (2,0,1)] then internally to [(1,1,0), (3,0,0), (2,0,1)] (since 2<3). Step4: pop returns (1,1,0), heap=[(2,0,1), (3,0,0)]. Step5: pop returns (2,0,1), heap=[(3,0,0)].

Heap init: []. Step1: push (5,0,0), heap=[(5,0,0)]. Step2: push (5,1,0), heap=[(5,0,0), (5,1,0)] (tie on 5, then 0<1). Step3: push (3,0,1), heap reorders to [(3,0,1), (5,1,0), (5,0,0)]. Step4: pop returns (3,0,1), heap=[(5,0,0), (5,1,0)]. Step5: pop returns (5,0,0), heap=[(5,1,0)]. Step6: pop returns (5,1,0), heap=[].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Min-Heap and Priority Queue Basics
ladders.desc: Introduction to min-heap data structure and its operations for beginners.
ladders.explanation: A min-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is smaller than or equal to its children (min-heap property). This ensures the smallest element is always at the root. In Python, we use the heapq module which provides heap operations on lists. Key concepts: 1. Heap is not sorted—it only guarantees root is minimum. 2. It supports efficient insert (push) and extract-min (pop) in O(log n) time. 3. We can heapify an existing list in O(n) time. Basic operations: heapq.heappush(heap, item) adds item; heapq.heappop(heap) removes and returns smallest item; heapq.heapify(x) transforms list x into heap in-place. Intuition: Think of a heap as a priority queue—always process the highest priority (smallest value) item first. Example: if you have tasks with priorities, heap lets you always get the most urgent task. For numbers, min-heap gives ascending order when popped repeatedly. Connection: This foundational knowledge is required for L4's tuple operations. Common mistakes: Assuming heap is sorted list, using wrong module (queue.PriorityQueue is alternative), not understanding O(log n) time. Real pattern: import heapq; heap = []; heapq.heappush(heap, 5); smallest = heapq.heappop(heap).

examples.trace: Heap init: []. Step1: push 5, heap=[5]. Step2: push 3, heap reorders to [3,5] (3 becomes root). Step3: push 8, heap=[3,5,8]. Step4: pop returns 3 (root), heap reheapifies to [5,8]. Step5: pop returns 5, heap=[8].

Heap init: []. Step1: push 10, heap=[10]. Step2: push 1, heap reorders to [1,10]. Step3: push 6, heap=[1,10,6] then reheapifies to [1,6,10] (since 6<10). Step4: pop returns 1, heap=[6,10]. Step5: pop returns 6, heap=[10]. Step6: pop returns 10, heap=[].
